(Spanish pronunciation: [[tʃiˈʝan]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")) is the capital [city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Chile "List of cities in Chile") of [Ñuble Region](/wiki/%C3%91uble_Region "Ñuble Region"), [Diguillín Province](/wiki/Diguill%C3%ADn_Province "Diguillín Province"), [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), located about 400 km (249 mi) south of the country's capital, [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago "Santiago"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) near the center of the country. It has been the capital of the new [Ñuble Region](/wiki/%C3%91uble_Region "Ñuble Region") since 6 September 2015. Within the city is a railway station, an [intercity bus](/wiki/Intercity_bus_service "Intercity bus service") terminal named María Teresa, and a regimental military base. The city features a modern, enclosed shopping centre in addition to the Chillán Market, an iconic multi-block, open-air [farmers' market](/wiki/Farmers%27_market "Farmers' market") and street fair where fruits, vegetables, crafts, clothing and other goods are sold. The nearby mountains, such as in [Laguna del Laja National Park](/wiki/Laguna_del_Laja_National_Park "Laguna del Laja National Park") ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Parque Nacional Laguna del Laja*) and the *[Nevados de Chillán](/wiki/Nevados_de_Chill%C3%A1n "Nevados de Chillán")* ([English](/wiki/English_language "English language"): 'snowy peaks of Chillán') are popular destinations for [skiing](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing"), hiking and [hot springs](/wiki/Hot_spring "Hot spring").

**Chillán** (Spanish pronunciation: [[tʃiˈʝan]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")) is the capital [city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Chile "List of cities in Chile") of [Ñuble Region](/wiki/%C3%91uble_Region "Ñuble Region"), [Diguillín Province](/wiki/Diguill%C3%ADn_Province "Diguillín Province"), [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), located about 400 km (249 mi) south of the country's capital, [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago "Santiago"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) near the center of the country. It has been the capital of the new [Ñuble Region](/wiki/%C3%91uble_Region "Ñuble Region") since 6 September 2015. Within the city is a railway station, an [intercity bus](/wiki/Intercity_bus_service "Intercity bus service") terminal named María Teresa, and a regimental military base. The city features a modern, enclosed shopping centre in addition to the Chillán Market, an iconic multi-block, open-air [farmers' market](/wiki/Farmers%27_market "Farmers' market") and street fair where fruits, vegetables, crafts, clothing and other goods are sold. The nearby mountains, such as in [Laguna del Laja National Park](/wiki/Laguna_del_Laja_National_Park "Laguna del Laja National Park") ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Parque Nacional Laguna del Laja*) and the *[Nevados de Chillán](/wiki/Nevados_de_Chill%C3%A1n "Nevados de Chillán")* ([English](/wiki/English_language "English language"): 'snowy peaks of Chillán') are popular destinations for [skiing](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing"), hiking and [hot springs](/wiki/Hot_spring "Hot spring").

Founded by the [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_conquest_of_the_Inca_Empire "Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire") in 1580, the city withstood numerous early attacks by the indigenous [Mapuche](/wiki/Mapuche "Mapuche") and [Pehuenche](/wiki/Pehuenche "Pehuenche"), among other peoples, who were vehemently [opposed to Spanish colonialism](/wiki/Arauco_War "Arauco War"). Over time, Chillán became an important marketplace and settlement area for spanish colonizers and their [Criollos](/wiki/Criollo_people "Criollo people") descendants. Many goods from [Patagonia](/wiki/Patagonia "Patagonia") and the [Argentine Pampas](/wiki/Pampas "Pampas") were brought into Chillán across the mountain passes of the area. In the early 19th century, the countryside of Chillán was ravaged by the [Chilean War of Independence](/wiki/Chilean_War_of_Independence "Chilean War of Independence") and a subsequent [banditry epidemic](/wiki/Banditry_in_Chile "Banditry in Chile").[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1939, the city was devastated by [a large earthquake](/wiki/1939_Chill%C3%A1n_earthquake "1939 Chillán earthquake"), prompting the government to initiate an extensive reconstruction program.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chill%C3%A1n&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The zone where Chillán was built was previously inhabited by indigenous people called [Chiquillanes](/w/index.php?title=Chiquillanes&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chiquillanes (page does not exist)").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

According to Friar [Ernesto Wilhelm de Moesbach](/w/index.php?title=Ernesto_Wilhelm_de_Moesbach&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ernesto Wilhelm de Moesbach (page does not exist)") Chillán is etymologically derived from "chilla", an indigenous word for the [South American gray fox](/wiki/South_American_gray_fox "South American gray fox").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Chillán was founded in 1580 at the site of [Chillán Viejo](/wiki/Chill%C3%A1n_Viejo "Chillán Viejo") as **San Bartolomé de Chillán** by [Martín Ruiz de Gamboa](/wiki/Mart%C3%ADn_Ruiz_de_Gamboa "Martín Ruiz de Gamboa"),[[9]](#cite_note-9)
who was campaigning against the local indigenous peoples at the time, but this moniker did not fare well, and was replaced by the current name, which in the local Indian language means "where the Sun is sitting".

During the [Mapuche uprising of 1655](/wiki/Mapuche_uprising_of_1655 "Mapuche uprising of 1655"), the city was besieged by Mapuche warriors.[[10]](#cite_note-barana352-10) The Spanish defended the city from trenches and a palisade fort.[[10]](#cite_note-barana352-10)[[11]](#cite_note-barana357-11) Hoping for a miracle, the Spaniards put an image of Mary near the trenches, against which Mapuches are said to have shot arrows.[[10]](#cite_note-barana352-10) In early March, about one month after the onset of uprising, distress was such that the Spaniards abandoned the city and headed north, escaping the conflict zone.[[11]](#cite_note-barana357-11) The [Real Audiencia of Santiago](/wiki/Real_Audiencia_of_Santiago "Real Audiencia of Santiago") declared the evacuation an act of cowardice, and prohibited refugees from Chillán to go beyond the [Maule River](/wiki/Maule_River "Maule River") north.[[12]](#cite_note-barana360-12) As an outbreak of [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox") occurred among the refugees, this was. in effect. a quarantine, as trespassing north was punished with death sentences.[[12]](#cite_note-barana360-12)

From its foundation, Chillán has been at the heart of Chile's rich agricultural region. It is also in a region of seismic activity, suffering from devastating earthquakes throughout its history; the [1939 Chillán earthquake](/wiki/1939_Chill%C3%A1n_earthquake "1939 Chillán earthquake") left over 30,000 dead and mobilized international help.

Chile's [founding father](/wiki/List_of_national_founders "List of national founders"), [Bernardo O'Higgins](/wiki/Bernardo_O%27Higgins "Bernardo O'Higgins"), was born in Chillán in 1778. He was the force behind [Chile's Independence](/wiki/Chilean_War_of_Independence "Chilean War of Independence") from [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), being elected [supreme director](/wiki/President_of_Chile "President of Chile") and declaring independence after the [Battle of Chacabuco](/wiki/Battle_of_Chacabuco "Battle of Chacabuco") against the Spaniards in 1817. His later victory at the [Maipú](/wiki/Battle_of_Maip%C3%BA "Battle of Maipú") battlefield cemented the country's freedom. He died in exile in [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru") in 1842.

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chill%C3%A1n&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Climate")]

Chillán has a [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate#Warm-summer_Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Csb*).[[13]](#cite_note-13) Winters are cool but mild, with a July average of 7.9 °C (46.2 °F). Most of the precipitation falls during this time of the year, with May to July being the wettest months, averaging over 200 mm (8 in).[[14]](#cite_note-DMC-14) Summers, though, are dry and warm, with a January average of 20.1 °C (68.2 °F), and during this time, precipitation is rare, averaging only 2–3 days per month from December to February. Temperatures can occasionally exceed 30 °C (86.0 °F) from October to April. The average annual precipitation is 1,058 mm (42 in), but it is highly variable from year to year, with 1982 being the wettest year at 1,813 mm (71 in) and 1998 being the driest year at only 473 mm (19 in).[[14]](#cite_note-DMC-14)

The air in Chillán is the fourth-most polluted in Chile, after [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago "Santiago"), [Temuco](/wiki/Temuco "Temuco"), and [Concepción](/wiki/Concepci%C3%B3n,_Chile "Concepción, Chile"). "As in Temuco, the main cause of air pollution in Chillán is the use of wood-burning stoves; about 62% of all households in Chillán use firewood as their main source of heating."[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chill%C3%A1n&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Demographics")]

According to the 2002 [census](/wiki/Census "Census") by the [National Statistics Institute](/wiki/National_Statistics_Institute_(Chile) "National Statistics Institute (Chile)"), the commune of Chillán spans an area of 511.2 km2 (197 sq mi) and has 161,953 inhabitants (77,007 men and 84,946 women). Of these, 148,015 (91.4%) lived in [urban areas](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") and 13,938 (8.6%) in [rural areas](/wiki/Rural_area "Rural area"). The population grew by 8.3% (12,442 persons) between the 1992 and 2002 censuses.[[3]](#cite_note-INE-3)

The [demonym](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") for a person from Chillán, used for more than 400 years by local residents, is *Chillanejo*, yet this is not found in the Royal Spanish Academy Dictionary, which only recognizes *Chillanense*.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chill%C3%A1n&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Notable people")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Claudio_Arrau_1_Allan_Warren.jpg/250px-Claudio_Arrau_1_Allan_Warren.jpg)](/wiki/File:Claudio_Arrau_1_Allan_Warren.jpg)

Claudio Arrau in 1974, by [Allan Warren](/wiki/Allan_Warren "Allan Warren")

[Isabel Riquelme](/wiki/Isabel_Riquelme "Isabel Riquelme"), mother of Chilean independence leader Bernardo O'Higgins, was born in Chillán in 1758. [María Cornelia Olivares](/wiki/Mar%C3%ADa_Cornelia_Olivares "María Cornelia Olivares"), one of the national heroines of the [Chilean War of Independence](/wiki/Chilean_War_of_Independence "Chilean War of Independence"), was also from the city. In addition, Chillán has produced a number of artists. A notable example is [Claudio Arrau](/wiki/Claudio_Arrau "Claudio Arrau"), a [pianist](/wiki/Pianist "Pianist"). Additionally, [Ramón Vinay](/wiki/Ram%C3%B3n_Vinay "Ramón Vinay") is the tenor who played [Otello](/wiki/Otello "Otello") in the 1950s, his recording of the role with Toscanini. He was a regular at the New York City [Metropolitan Opera](/wiki/Metropolitan_Opera "Metropolitan Opera"), where he sang both tenor and baritone roles. One of his last performances at this house was as the [Barber of Seville](/wiki/Barber_of_Seville "Barber of Seville")'s Basilio, a bass role. He retired from the stage in 1969.

Other "Chillanejos" include writer [Marta Brunet](/wiki/Marta_Brunet "Marta Brunet"), sculptor [Marta Colvin](/wiki/Marta_Colvin "Marta Colvin"), painter [Pacheco Altamirano](/wiki/Pacheco_Altamirano "Pacheco Altamirano"), and [Juan de Dios Aldea](/wiki/Juan_de_Dios_Aldea "Juan de Dios Aldea"), who, however, did not reach the international acclaim achieved by Arrau and Vinay. *[Super Smash Bros.](/wiki/Super_Smash_Bros. "Super Smash Bros.")* player [Gonzalo "ZeRo" Barrios](/wiki/Gonzalo_%22ZeRo%22_Barrios "Gonzalo \"ZeRo\" Barrios") who had a record-breaking 56-tournament winning streak is also from Chillán.

## Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chill%C3%A1n&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Administration")]

As a commune, Chillán is a third-level [administrative division of Chile](/wiki/Administrative_division_of_Chile "Administrative division of Chile") administered by a [municipal council](/wiki/Municipal_council "Municipal council"), headed by an *[alcalde](/wiki/Alcalde "Alcalde")*, who is directly elected every four years. The 2008-2020 *alcalde* is [Sergio Zarzar Andonie](/wiki/Sergio_Zarzar_Andonie "Sergio Zarzar Andonie") (ILE).[[1]](#cite_note-Official-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Munitel-2)

Within the [electoral divisions of Chile](/wiki/Electoral_divisions_of_Chile "Electoral divisions of Chile"), Chillán is represented in the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_Chile "Chamber of Deputies of Chile") by Carlos Abel Jarpa ([PRSD](/wiki/Social_Democrat_Radical_Party "Social Democrat Radical Party")) and Rosauro Martínez ([RN](/wiki/National_Renewal_(Chile) "National Renewal (Chile)")) as part of the 41st electoral district, together with [Coihueco](/wiki/Coihueco "Coihueco"), [Pinto](/wiki/Pinto,_Chile "Pinto, Chile"), [San Ignacio](/wiki/San_Ignacio,_Chile "San Ignacio, Chile"), [El Carmen](/wiki/El_Carmen,_Chile "El Carmen, Chile"), [Pemuco](/wiki/Pemuco "Pemuco"), [Yungay](/wiki/Yungay,_Chile "Yungay, Chile") and [Chillán Viejo](/wiki/Chill%C3%A1n_Viejo "Chillán Viejo"). The commune is represented in the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Chile "Senate of Chile") by Victor Pérez Varela ([UDI](/wiki/Independent_Democratic_Union "Independent Democratic Union")) and [Felipe Harboe](/wiki/Felipe_Harboe "Felipe Harboe") ([PPD](/wiki/Party_for_Democracy_(Chile) "Party for Democracy (Chile)")) as part of the 13th senatorial constituency (Biobío-Coast).

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chill%C3%A1n&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Economy")]

In 2021, the number of registered companies in Chillán was 14,689.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The Economic Complexity Index (ECI) in 2018 was 2.02, while the economic activities with the highest Revealed Comparative Advantage (RCA) indices were Repair of Agricultural and Forestry Machinery (140.3), Other Sports-Related Activities (45.83), and Retail Sale of Lamps, Wall Lights, and Similar Items (20.48).

## International Relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chill%C3%A1n&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: International Relations")]

The city of Chillán hosts a series of international relations institutions, such as the Regional Unit of International Affairs (URAI) of the Regional Government of Ñuble, responsible for the analysis and management of the region’s bilateral and multilateral relations with Latin America and the rest of the world; the Regional Unit for Investment Promotion and Attraction; the regional offices of the National Migration Service and the General Directorate for Export Promotion (ProChile); the Department of Migration and International Police of the Investigations Police of Chile; and the Office of Social and Cultural Diversity of the Municipality of Chillán, which provides support to the city’s migrant population.[[22]](#cite_note-22)
In the field of international relations and higher education, the main actor in Chillán is the International Relations Unit of the Office of Outreach and Engagement, together with the Institute of Languages of the Adventist University of Chile.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chill%C3%A1n&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Transport")]

The city of Chillán is connected to Chile's capital Santiago by both a modern highway and a rebuilt railway system [TerraSur](/wiki/TerraSur "TerraSur") that makes the trip in less than five hours. TerraSur, which terminates in Chillán station, and the  [Alameda-Temuco train](/wiki/Alameda_railway_station "Alameda railway station") both operate on the railway connecting Chillan with [Rancagua](/wiki/Rancagua "Rancagua") and [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago "Santiago").
